text: The surgeon places robotic ports in the abdominal wall, preparing for the ileocolic resection procedure.
The surgeon initiates dissection by creating a plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully separating the tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube by making precise cuts along the medial peritoneum, facilitating the separation of the rectal tube from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon reflects the rectosigmoid and descending colon off the retroperitoneum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery to its root, clips it, and divides it beyond the origin of the left colic artery. This is followed by the posterior pelvic dissection of the rectal tube.
The surgeon completes lateral peritoneal cuts, extending them over the external iliac vessels to facilitate the dissection of lateral pelvic lymph nodes.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the external iliac vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects the ureter and shifts it medially, ensuring its separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon traces the obliterated umbilical artery towards the internal iliac vasculature, revealing the anatomical pathway and connections between these structures.
The surgeon creates a paravascular space adjacent to the dissected inferior mesenteric artery, facilitating further surgical access and manipulation in the pelvic region.
The surgeon dissects and removes the nodal tissue located between the obturator internus muscle and the internal iliac vasculature.
The surgeon addresses the medial nodal zone, following the dissection of the lateral nodal zone, focusing on the nodal tissue adjacent to the internal iliac vasculature.
The surgical field shows the zone between the internal iliac vasculature on the lateral side and the pelvic hypogastric region on the medial side.

The surgeon creates a space through gentle dissection, carefully separating and removing the nodal tissue that is medial to the internal iliac vasculature from the branches of the internal iliac vessels.
The surgeon clips and divides the superior vesicle pedicle, then proceeds to clip and divide the blood vessels supplying the prostate and seminal vesicles.
The surgeon clips and divides the left ureter near the bladder, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects tissue off the retroperitoneum to facilitate the entry of the ureter for anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a right lateral pelvic node dissection, using similar techniques as previously described. The right ureter is clipped, divided, and mobilized, with careful manipulation to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder, separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further surgical steps.
The surgeon divides both umbilical arteries and proceeds to take down the bladder by entering the space of Retzius on the right side.
The surgeon identifies the dorsal venous complex, clips it, and divides it, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects the prostate and lifts it off the pelvic floor, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon controls bleeding from the dorsal venous complex and packs the area to maintain a clean surgical field.
The surgeon divides the descending colon using an endostapler and clips the descending colon mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall, creating space for the ureteral anastomosis.
The surgeon mobilizes the ileocecal junction, preparing the area for the placement of the ileal conduit.
The surgeon uses umbilical tape to measure the bowel length, marking the proximal and distal ends of the conduit with clips.
The surgeon uses endo staplers to divide the bowel, separating the tissue cleanly along the intended line of resection.
The surgeon establishes bowel continuity by performing a side-to-side ileo-ileostomy using endo staplers, joining the ileal segments.
The surgeon spatulates the cut ends of the ureters and joins their medial walls using absorbable suture material, ensuring precise alignment for the anastomosis.
The surgeon inserts the digest ends into both ureters using a guide wire, while the other ends are placed into the distal end of the ileal conduit.
The surgeon completes the ureteroenteric anastomosis by applying continuous absorbable sutures to securely join the ureters to the bowel conduit.
The surgeon completes the final part of the excision through the perineal route, indicating the conclusion of the surgical resection.
The surgeon fashions the ileal conduit and the end colostomy, shaping and securing these structures within the surgical field.
